name: Peal de Becerro – tour around Cazorla and Castillo de la Yedra
name_original: Peal de Becerro – vuelta por Cazorla y Castillo de la Yedra
region_intro: Road cycling around Peal De Becerro offers diverse landscapes, transitioning from extensive olive groves and cereal fields to mountainous terrain. The region is characterized by a varied topography, with a significant portion lying within the Sierras de Cazorla, Segura y Las Villas Natural Park. This area features an "orographic labyrinth" of valleys, gorges, and mountains, providing challenging climbs and rewarding descents for road cyclists. Roads wind through areas of hydrological importance with numerous rivers and streams, alongside diverse botanical significance. Best road cycling routes around Peal De Becerro The most popular road cycling route is Cazorla – tour around the Natural Park of Sierra de Cazorla, Segura y las Villas, a 85.9 miles (138.3 km) trail that takes 8 hours 11 minutes to complete. This difficult route explores the vast protected area of the Natural Park, featuring significant elevation gains. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Peal de Becerro – tour around Cazorla and Castillo de la Yedra, a moderate 23.2 miles (37.4 km) path. This route offers views of historical landmarks and varied terrain around Peal de Becerro and Cazorla. Local road cyclists also love the Río de la Vega by Santo Tomé and Peal de Becerro — circular from Quesada, a 33.9 miles (54.5 km) trail leading through agricultural landscapes and riverside areas, often completed in about 2 hours 51 minutes. Road cycling around Peal De Becerro is defined by extensive olive groves, mountainous terrain within the Sierras de Cazorla Natural Park, and winding roads through valleys and gorges. The network offers options for easy, moderate, and difficult routes, catering to various ability levels. The routes in Peal De Becerro are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 5.0 stars from over 10 reviews. More than 1400 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Peal De Becerro's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on road cycling routes around Peal de Becerro?
A: Road cycling around Peal de Becerro offers a diverse landscape. You'll find routes traversing extensive olive groves and cereal fields, providing picturesque agricultural scenery. To the east, the terrain becomes more mountainous as it enters the Sierras de Cazorla, Segura y Las Villas Natural Park, featuring challenging climbs and rewarding descents through valleys, gorges, and mountains.

Q: Are there routes suitable for different skill levels?
A: Yes, the region caters to various abilities. Out of 54 available road cycling routes, there are 2 easy routes, 20 moderate routes, and 32 difficult routes. This variety ensures options for beginners looking for gentler rides and experienced cyclists seeking challenging ascents.

Q: What are some notable natural features I might encounter on a road cycling tour?
A: The Sierras de Cazorla, Segura y Las Villas Natural Park is a prominent feature, known for its hydrological importance with numerous rivers and streams, and diverse botanical significance. You might also encounter impressive rocky cliffs at Loma de Castellones or the riverside vegetation around Tornillos de Gualay. For a stunning viewpoint, consider routes that offer glimpses of &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/183641" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Pico Gilillo&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Can I find any historical landmarks or attractions along the cycling routes?
A: Absolutely. Peal de Becerro and its surroundings are rich in history. You could cycle past the ancient Iberian burial chamber, &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5297752" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cámara Sepulcral de Toya&lt;/a&gt;, or see the medieval &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e996066334/peal-de-becerro-tour-around-cazorla-and-castillo-de-la-yedra" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Torre del Reloj and Torre Mocha&lt;/a&gt; in the urban center. The Roman &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3983035" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Acueducto de Hornos de Peal&lt;/a&gt; also adds historical charm to the area.

Q: Are there any circular road cycling routes available?
A: Yes, there are several circular routes. One popular option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1344749104/rio-de-la-vega-by-santo-tome-and-peal-de-becerro-circular-from-quesada" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Río de la Vega by Santo Tomé and Peal de Becerro — circular from Quesada&lt;/a&gt;, which is a moderate 33.9-mile (54.5 km) trail through agricultural landscapes and riverside areas. Another is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1345173203/alto-guadalquivir-and-jandulilla-river-loop-from-peal-de-becerro" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Alto Guadalquivir and Jandulilla River Loop from Peal de Becerro&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What is the best time of year for road cycling in Peal de Becerro?
A: Peal de Becerro is an excellent destination for outdoor activities. The varied topography and natural park setting suggest pleasant conditions during spring and autumn, when temperatures are milder. Summer can be quite warm, especially in the olive groves, while winter might offer cooler, more challenging rides in the mountainous areas.

Q: What do other road cyclists say about the routes in Peal de Becerro?
A: The road cycling routes in Peal de Becerro are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 5.0 stars from over 10 reviews. Over 1400 road cyclists have used komoot to explore the region's varied terrain, often praising the diverse landscapes and the combination of challenging climbs and scenic descents.

Q: Are there any long-distance or challenging road cycling routes?
A: For those seeking a significant challenge, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e996055782/cazorla-tour-around-the-natural-park-of-sierra-de-cazorla-segura-y-las-villas" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cazorla – tour around the Natural Park of Sierra de Cazorla, Segura y las Villas&lt;/a&gt; is a difficult 85.9-mile (138.3 km) trail with significant elevation gains, exploring the vast protected area. Another difficult option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1343627302/portillo-reservoir-and-hoya-de-baza-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Portillo Reservoir and Hoya de Baza Loop&lt;/a&gt;, covering 70.1 miles (112.8 km).

Q: Where can I find places to rest or refuel along the routes?
A: While specific cafes or pubs directly on every route are not listed, Peal de Becerro and nearby towns like Cazorla offer various amenities. It's advisable to plan your route to pass through these towns for refreshment stops. The region's focus on tourism means you'll find options for food and drink in populated areas.

Q: Are there any specific permits required for cycling in the Sierras de Cazorla, Segura y Las Villas Natural Park?
A: Generally, road cycling on established public roads within the Natural Park does not require special permits. However, if you plan to venture onto specific trails or protected areas that might be restricted, it's always best to check with the park's official administration for any specific regulations or permits that might apply to certain zones.

Q: What kind of accommodation is available for cyclists in Peal de Becerro?
A: Peal de Becerro and its surrounding areas offer various accommodation options, including hotels, guesthouses, and rural lodgings. Given its proximity to a major natural park, many establishments cater to outdoor enthusiasts, and some may offer facilities like secure bike storage. It's recommended to check with individual accommodations for cyclist-specific amenities.

Q: Are there any family-friendly road cycling options in the area?
A: While many routes in the mountainous park are challenging, the region also features gentler terrain through olive groves and cereal fields. Look for routes designated as 'easy' or 'moderate' that avoid significant elevation changes, particularly those closer to the town center or along flatter agricultural paths, which can be more suitable for families.
tips:
La Yedra Castle is an ancient defensive structure in the Spanish municipality of Cazorla. It is located at the foot of the Salvatierra hill, above the Cerezuelo river, in the province of Jaén. It is a building from the Berber period, but it was completed in its current form by the Castilians. From the complex, located 831 meters above sea level, you can see the city of Cazorla and its surroundings.

The architecture has several Muslim features, although over the centuries Christians completed the final design, so the Gothic style predominates (translated from Wikipedia).

Stone tower remains of a medieval castle, The Mocha tower, is seven meters high. It is believed that its construction took place between the 13th and 14th centuries. Located in the center of Peal de Becerro. Good place for a quick coffee or a cold drink.

waypoints.name: Torre Mocha of Peal de Becerro
